petitioner_background: The petitioner is a scientist with a background in molecular biology who was also pursuing a legal career. He earned a Master of Science in Biotechnology from Aligarh University (India) in 1987 and a Ph.D. in Molecular Biology from Hokkaido University (Japan) in 1994, and at the time of filing was a full-time law student at Suffolk University Law School in Boston, later receiving his J.D. in 2005. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, with prior research experience at Novartis, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Massachusetts General Hospital, and Rhode Island Hospital.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) three certificates from Hokkaido University indicating receipt of a Monbusho (Japanese Government) Scholarship, including one dated November 19, 1999, certifying his status as a doctoral student from April 1, 1991 to March 24, 1994; (2) a document reflecting the Japanese Government's 2004 criteria for the scholarship, which required applicants to be under 35 years of age and university graduates with 16 years of schooling, and focused on academic excellence and research planning; (3) a 'Certificate of Merit' issued by Pharmacia Biotech and the journal Science 'in recognition of the high standard of work entered in competition for the Pharmacia Biotech & Science Prize for Young Scientists 1995'; (4) information from the AAAS website describing the award process (top five essays per region forwarded to judges, up to three winners per region, regional winners competing for a $25,000 grand prize); (5) the 'Rules of Eligibility' indicating the prize recognized only work performed while the entrant was a graduate student; and (6) award certificates from the Zoological Society of Aligarh University and the Literacy and Culture Society of Aligarh University.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found none of the awards qualifying. The Monbusho Scholarship criteria did not require 'excellence in the field of endeavor'—university study is training for future employment, not a field of endeavor itself, and the scholarship was presented for scholastic achievement and pursuit of academic study. Competition was limited to students under 35 rather than experienced professionals, so an individual selected for a graduate scholarship cannot be said to stand at the very top of the field, and obtaining financial support for academic studies does not constitute a nationally or internationally recognized prize. The Pharmacia Biotech/Science Certificate of Merit only acknowledged that the petitioner 'entered' the competition, with no evidence he was named a regional or grand prize winner, and the prize was limited to work performed as a graduate student. The Aligarh University awards reflected only local or institutional recognition rather than national recognition. The AAO rejected counsel's argument that these awards showed international recognition, finding no meaningful comparison to experienced scientists in the field and noting that recognition within an 'approximate age group' or among 'promising scientists at the beginning of their careers' does not indicate reaching the very top of the field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his membership in the Boston Patent Law Association (BPLA), including information from the BPLA website stating that 'ACTIVE MEMBERS must be attorneys at law who are in good standing in any Court of record in the United States, or any of the States or territories thereof, and engaged in the practice of patent, trademark, copyright or other intellectual property law and who reside or have a principle office in the First Federal Judicial Circuit.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence that admission to BPLA membership required outstanding achievement or that the petitioner was evaluated by nationally or internationally recognized experts. Membership requirements based on employment or activity in a given field, minimum education or experience, standardized test scores, grade point average, recommendations, or payment of dues do not satisfy this criterion. Members must also be selected at the national or international level, not local or regional, and the overall prestige of the association is not determinative—the issue is membership requirements rather than reputation. The BPLA's geographic limitation to the First Federal Judicial Circuit and its practice-based admission standards failed this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, the petitioner submitted letters from five of his law school professors at Suffolk University, his former supervisor at a Boston firm, a former co-worker from the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute (where he worked 1999-2000), a partner from a Boston law firm where he served as a legal intern, and his current superior. A letter from Dr. [redacted], Professor Emeritus at Hokkaido University, stated the petitioner worked under his supervision for more than 6 years and 'performed highly innovative work and help[ed] to shape the field of the research in understanding how the transcription of a cancer-causing gene, hst (also known as fibroblast growth factor-4) is developmentally and transcriptionally regulated in embryonic stem cell carcinoma,' and that the petitioner published his Ph.D. thesis in a peer-reviewed U.S. journal. A letter from Dr. [redacted], Chief Scientist at Novartis Institutes for Biomedical Research (Tsukuba, Japan), stated the petitioner worked at Novartis until June 1997, made valuable contributions in elucidating mechanisms of cancer, cancer metastasis, osteoporosis, and transcriptional regulation, and published work in Biochemical and Biophysical Research Communications. The petitioner also submitted evidence of conference presentations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters detailed the petitioner's educational background and employment but provided no information on how his work significantly influenced the greater field. Acceptance of a scientific manuscript for publication merely means it offered new and useful information—it does not follow that every graduate student whose research is published has made a contribution of major significance. The Novartis publication fell under the separate 'scholarly articles' criterion, and because criteria are distinct, evidence meeting one does not satisfy another. Regarding conference presentations, acclaim in science is generally not established by merely presenting work at conferences; participation in symposia is routine in the scientific community and does not elevate the petitioner above others. The witnesses all consisted of individuals with whom the petitioner studied or worked, which is not first-hand evidence of acclaim outside affiliated institutions. The AAO concluded the petitioner's contributions did not far exceed those of other capable biomedical researchers or patent attorneys.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of authorship of articles appearing in The Journal of Biological Chemistry and Biochemical and Biophysical Research Communications. On appeal, he submitted a citation index showing his Journal of Biological Chemistry article had been cited approximately a dozen times.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence adequate to minimally satisfy this criterion. However, in a footnote the AAO noted that while the citation history demonstrated a degree of interest in one article, the petitioner had not shown that the citation frequency of his body of work over the last decade elevated him above almost all others in his field. The AAO accepted he had authored a few published papers, but the weight was diminished by lack of evidence showing the findings were significantly influential.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a March 11, 1997 letter from Dr. [redacted], Assistant Professor, Cutaneous Biology Research Center/Massachusetts General Hospital, Harvard Medical School, offering a Research Fellow in Dermatology position at $24,000/year; (2) a November 4, 1998 letter from Dr. [redacted], Associate Dean of Medicine at Brown University, appointing the petitioner as Research Fellow in Pathology at Rhode Island Hospital for three years (January 1, 1999 to December 31, 2001); (3) a December 11, 2000 memorandum from Dr. [redacted], Business Manager for Research at Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, confirming employment as a 'Research Associate' in the Department of Cancer Immunology and AIDS from December 1, 1999 to December 31, 2000; and (4) a letter from Dr. [redacted], now Associate Director of Business Development at Genzyme Genetics, who briefly worked at Dana-Farber with the petitioner and stated the petitioner gained scientific work experience at prestigious commercial and academic institutions including Dana-Farber Cancer Institute.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no letters of support originating from the institutions themselves explaining the importance of the petitioner's role. Dr. [redacted]'s letter stated only that the petitioner 'gained scientific work experience' at Dana-Farber, not that he performed in a leading or critical role. The petitioner's roles as Research Fellow or Research Associate are subordinate roles providing temporary scientific training for a future professional career. The record included no evidence showing the extent of the petitioner's substantial control over personnel or research decisions. While the AAO accepted the institutions had distinguished reputations, there was no evidence the petitioner's role was of significantly greater importance than that of other researchers (including tenured faculty). The evidence failed to demonstrate performance in a leading or critical role or that involvement earned sustained national or international acclaim.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO found the evidence satisfied only one of the regulatory criteria (scholarly articles) at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3), whereas at least three must be met. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself to such an extent that he had achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. The AAO also rejected counsel's attempt to have the petition alternatively adjudicated as a National Interest Waiver under 203(b)(2), finding that a petitioner is not entitled to multiple adjudications for a single petition with a single fee. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner, who did not sustain it, and the appeal was dismissed.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: The petitioner and counsel attempted to have a single I-140 petition adjudicated under multiple classifications—both as an alien of extraordinary ability under 203(b)(1)(A) and alternatively as a member of the professions holding advanced degrees qualifying for a National Interest Waiver under 203(b)(2). The AAO held that a petitioner is not entitled to multiple adjudications and decisions for a single petition with a single fee, citing 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(a)(1) and the I-140 form instructions requiring the petitioner to 'check one' classification. Consequently, the AAO limited its discussion to eligibility under 203(b)(1)(A) only.
